pages.full_text:
only a prior, 2 monks + lots of servants. We drove on to Craster + parked + walked to Dunstanborough + back - glorious sunshine. We've come back to the same lovely little campsite, Budle farm.

[photo of Dunstanburgh Castle from aerial view]

Dunstanburgh Castle. Early C4. Lancastrian Stronghold during Wars of the Roses. Built by a nobleman to show off to the king.
photo_captions: Dunstanburgh Castle. Early C4. Lancastrian Stronghold during Wars of the Roses. Built by a nobleman to show off to the king.
pages.notes: The castle name appears as 'Dunstanborough' in the narrative text and 'Dunstanburgh' in the photo caption — both transcribed as written. The photo caption is written vertically along the right edge of the photo in green ink. 'C4' likely means 'C14' (14th century) but transcribed as written.

pages.full_text:
__Tuesday__
   We returned to Lindisfarne to see the Castle, hoping to see the Castle in the morning & then go on to Cragside in the afternoon. However the Castle didn't open until 12. So we packed a lunch + walked around the island, seeing Jekell's beautiful garden. We arrived at the castle at 12 but there was a huge queue, so we decided to return in winter.
   When we reached Cragside, near Rothbury, there was again a huge queue to get into the house. So we saw the Iron Bridge & lakes & Pumphouse & Simon walked to the Powerhouse where Lord Armstrong made hydro electric power. Now we have come on to __Snitter Farm__ __Campsite__, an open view of woods & fields. Tonic is getting better, gradually allowed to eat more but dreadful smells
   Simon & I are sitting in a howling gale + weeping with laughter because his farts are so bad.
pages.notes: The name 'Jekell's' likely refers to Gertrude Jekyll, the garden designer associated with Lindisfarne Castle garden — transcribed as written. 'Tonic' appears to be a pet (dog). No year is visible on this page; volume B covers 2003–2019 but no further anchoring evidence is present on this spread. Page number 21 is visible at top-right.
